title: Chapter 3: Mass, Weight and Density — PYP

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The mass of an object does not change with location.
The weight of an object can be measured with a beam balance.
The mass of an object can be measured with a spring balance.
The weight of an object can never change.
questions.explanation:
Mass is a measure of the amount of matter and is constant. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking weight is measured with a beam balance; a beam balance is used for comparing masses/weights.

questions.question: A rock on the Moon has a mass of $0.5\text{ kg}$. It is brought to the Earth, where the gravitational field strength is stronger. On the Earth, the rock will have:
options: the same mass and more weight.
less mass and the same weight.
the same mass and the same weight.
questions.explanation:
Mass is constant ($0.5\text{ kg}$). Because Earth's $g$ is stronger than the Moon's, the weight ($W=mg$) will be greater on Earth. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the mass will increase because the 'weight' feels heavier.

questions.question: Which statement correctly defines weight?
options: A gravitational force on an object that has mass
A measure of the quantity of matter in an object
The force per unit mass in a gravitational field
The resistance of an object to a change in its motion
questions.explanation:
Weight is defined as the effect of a gravitational field on a mass, resulting in a force. 

**Common mistake:** Confusing the definition of weight with the definition of mass (quantity of matter).

questions.question: Planetary data shows that Venus has a gravitational field strength $g$ very similar to Earth's ($10\text{ N/kg}$). If an explorer has a weight of $800\text{ N}$ on Earth, what is the best estimate for their weight on Venus?
questions.explanation:
Since Venus has a $g$ value similar to Earth's, the explorer's weight ($W=mg$) will be close to their Earth weight ($800\text{ N}$). $720\text{ N}$ is the most plausible estimate among the choices. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting $800\text{ kg}$, which incorrectly uses mass units for weight.

questions.question: Gravitational field strength $g$ is equivalent to which other physical quantity for an object in free-fall?
questions.explanation:
Gravitational field strength $g$ is numerically equivalent to the acceleration of free fall in a uniform gravitational field. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking it is equivalent to the resultant force, which only equals $g$ if the mass is exactly $1\text{ kg}$ ($F = mg$).

questions.question: A student uses a measuring cylinder to find the volume of ten identical steel balls. The water level rises from $20\text{ cm}^3$ to $50\text{ cm}^3$. If one ball has a mass of $27\text{ g}$, what is the density of the steel?
questions.explanation:
Volume of 10 balls $= 30\text{ cm}^3$, so volume of 1 ball $= 3\text{ cm}^3$. Density $= 27\text{ g} / 3\text{ cm}^3 = 9.0\text{ g/cm}^3$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing the total mass of one ball by the total volume of ten balls ($0.9$).

questions.question: The table shows the densities of four different liquids. Which liquid will float on top of all the others if they are immiscible?
questions.explanation:
The liquid with the lowest density will always float on top of denser liquids. 

**Common mistake:** Choosing the liquid with the highest numerical value, confusing 'top layer' with 'most substance'.

questions.question: A graph is plotted with Mass on the y-axis and Volume on the x-axis for a particular substance. What does the gradient of the straight line represent?
questions.explanation:
Gradient $= \text{change in } y / \text{change in } x = \text{Mass} / \text{Volume}$, which is the definition of density. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the gradient is weight, which would require a Mass vs Weight graph.

questions.question: Which set of equipment is required to determine the density of an irregularly shaped piece of metal that sinks in water?
questions.explanation:
An electronic balance measures mass and a measuring cylinder (using displacement) measures the volume of irregular objects. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting a metre rule, which can only measure regular shapes easily.

questions.question: A block of wood has a density of $600\text{ kg/m}^3$. A second block of the same wood has double the mass. What is the density of the second block?
questions.explanation:
Density is a constant property for a uniform material regardless of mass or size. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking that doubling the mass also doubles the density.
